title: Returning spent mushroom substrate to the compost heap
subtitle: How to incorporate exhausted mushroom blocks and bags into the compost cycle
excerpt: A spent oyster mushroom block or straw log has produced its fruiting flushes and now carries colonised mycelium, spent cellulose, and a small amount of remaining nitrogen. Returned to a compost heap, it breaks down quickly and contributes fungal inoculant to the pile. The C:N ratio of spent substrate sits around 40:1 to 60:1, so it needs a balancing addition of nitrogen-rich greens.
sourceNotes: Stamets: Mycelium Running (2005) on fungal decomposition in compost. WRAP household composting guidelines for mixed organic materials. Research literature on fungal community succession in thermophilic compost piles.

glossaryTerms.definition: Grain, sawdust, or straw colonised with mushroom mycelium, used to inoculate a fresh substrate.
The ratio of carbon to nitrogen in compost material. Mushroom substrate is carbon-rich; requires balancing with green nitrogen material.

content.text: A 2 kg oyster mushroom block, inoculated from grain
and grown out on straw or hardwood sawdust, has a

of around 50:1 after the fruiting flushes are done. The mycelium has digested much of the available lignin and cellulose but has not broken the material down to a usable carbon form. Added directly to a hot compost heap, the block rehydrates and breaks down within 4 to 6 weeks in warm weather. The fungal mycelium provides a head start for fungal community succession in the compost.
What substrate is safe to compost
Oyster mushroom blocks (straw or hardwood sawdust): easy to compost. The mycelium has already started breaking down the woody material.
Wine cap (Stropharia) substrate: wood chip or straw that has hosted a bed of wine cap mushrooms. Already well-broken-down by year 2 or 3 of the bed's life; can be used as mulch directly or composted.
Shiitake logs: once the log no longer produces fruiting bodies, it can be shredded and added to the pile. Whole unshredded logs decompose slowly over years in the compost heap.
Break the block into pieces roughly 50 to 100 mm across. Smaller pieces increase surface area and speed breakdown.

Layer with nitrogen-rich green material to balance the C:N ratio: fresh grass clippings, kitchen vegetable scraps, coffee grounds, or fresh nettle. A ratio of 1 part substrate (by weight) to 2 parts greens (by fresh weight) approximately balances the C:N.

Water if dry. Spent substrate that dried out during the end of its fruiting life can be stiff and water-resistant; soaking for 30 minutes before adding it to the pile accelerates the restart of decomposition.
Using spent mushroom compost in the garden

Commercially available spent mushroom compost (SMC) is a separate product, typically composted straw from industrial button mushroom production. It has a pH of 7 to 8 and is calcium-rich from the casing lime. Homemade spent oyster substrate that has been hot-composted for a full season has a more neutral pH and a richer microbial profile. It is appropriate as a bed top-dressing or potting compost amendment at up to 30 percent by volume in a potting mix.
